title: Does CLIP’s generalization performance mainly stem from high train-test similarity?

reviews.summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.
reviews.strengths: The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.
reviews.weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.
reviews.questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.

Strengths:
The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.

Weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.

Questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates the fundamental question of whether CLIP's exceptional zero-shot generalization performance on out-of-distribution (OOD) benchmarks stems primarily from the presence of highly similar images in its massive training dataset (LAION-400M), or whether other properties of the training data are responsible. The authors note that CLIP achieves remarkably high accuracy on OOD datasets like ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R compared to models trained on ImageNet, which raises an important question: are these datasets truly out-of-distribution with respect to LAION-400M, or does the web-scale dataset simply contain many semantically and stylistically similar samples?

To address this, the authors propose that if CLIP's performance were mainly driven by high train-test similarity, then removing highly similar training samples should substantially degrade performance. The paper operationalizes this hypothesis through a series of carefully designed experiments that progressively refine the notion of "highly similar images" and test their impact on model performance.

The authors define perceptual similarity using cosine similarity in CLIP ViT-B/16+'s embedding space and demonstrate that this metric correlates well with visual closeness. They find empirically that LAION-400M contains more similar images to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R than ImageNet-Train does, supporting the premise that these benchmarks may not be truly OOD with respect to LAION. However, their key finding is surprising: when they prune LAION to match ImageNet's similarity distribution to these OOD benchmarks, CLIP still maintains substantially higher performance than ImageNet-trained models, leading them to conclude that high train-test similarity alone cannot explain CLIP's generalization.

## Strengths

The paper is exceptionally well written with clear motivation and logical flow. Each section builds naturally on the previous one, making the progression from initial observation to final conclusion easy to follow. The authors carefully define their terminology and explicitly state the assumptions underlying their methodology, which enhances reproducibility and clarity.

The experimental design is thorough and principled. Rather than making a single argument, the authors employ multiple complementary approaches to test their hypothesis. The progression from simple near/far-pruning experiments (Section 4.2) to the more sophisticated similarity gap framework (Section 4.4) demonstrates methodological sophistication. This layered approach allows the authors to isolate specific effects and build confidence in their conclusions.

The formalization of "highly similar images" through the similarity gap concept (Section 4.4) is particularly elegant. By defining highly similar images as those more similar to the test set than any image in ImageNet-Train, the authors create a principled and interpretable definition that enables surgical removal of specific training samples. This is superior to arbitrary similarity thresholds and allows for fair comparison between training sets of different sizes and compositions.

The paper provides compelling visual evidence through nearest-neighbor visualizations (Figures 1, 2, and the extensive appendix). These visualizations effectively communicate the core observation that LAION-400M contains perceptually similar neighbors to OOD test sets, while ImageNet-Train contains more dissimilar ones. The visualizations make the similarity metric's validity apparent even to readers unfamiliar with embedding spaces.

The practical contribution of the 100M LAION core set (Section 4.2 and Appendix B.4) is valuable for the community. By identifying a quarter-sized subset of LAION that maintains full performance on OOD benchmarks, the authors provide a resource that could facilitate future research into training data properties while reducing computational requirements.

The experimental controls are generally strong. The authors include appropriate baselines, such as random pruning (rand-pruned) as a control for the effect of dataset size reduction, which helps isolate the specific impact of similarity-based pruning. The comparison between near-pruning and far-pruning curves in Figure 3 effectively demonstrates that the performance degradation is specifically due to removing similar samples, not simply reducing dataset size.

The reproducibility statement and released code enhance the paper's contribution to the community. The detailed experimental specifications in Section 3 and the extensive appendices (particularly Sections B-G) provide sufficient detail for reproduction or extension of this work.

## Weaknesses

A significant limitation is that the experimental analysis focuses almost exclusively on ImageNet-based OOD benchmarks (ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-V2, and ObjectNet). While the paper does include some analysis of CelebA and Waterbirds in Appendix D, these additional experiments are relatively brief and not integrated into the main narrative. The core findings are based entirely on variations of ImageNet, which raises important questions about the generalizability of the conclusions.

This limitation is particularly concerning because ImageNet-based benchmarks represent a specific type of distribution shift—primarily stylistic variations (sketches, artistic renderings, unusual viewpoints) of objects that share the same semantic classes as the training data. It remains unclear whether the findings hold for other types of OOD scenarios. For instance, domain shift benchmarks like iWILDCam (7.45% zero-shot accuracy) or FMoW (12.96% zero-shot accuracy) involve fundamentally different distribution shifts—geographic and ecological variations rather than stylistic changes. The authors acknowledge this limitation in Appendix C and justify their focus on ImageNet, but the justification is somewhat circular: they choose ImageNet because CLIP performs well on it and because distribution shifts are "more human aligned." This reasoning doesn't address whether their core findings about similarity-driven performance extend to domains where CLIP performs poorly.

The choice of CLIP ViT-B/16+ as the similarity metric, while justified in Appendix E, raises methodological concerns. The authors use a specific pre-trained CLIP model to compute embeddings both for pruning the training data and for training the final models (ViT-B/32). This creates a subtle circularity: the similarity metric is based on a model that was itself trained on LAION-400M. While the appendix shows that using ViT-L-14 embeddings produces similar results (Table 5, Figure 13), the ablation is limited. More importantly, the correlation in Table 5 (ranging from 0.80 to 0.93) indicates that the choice of embedding does matter, and the paper doesn't explore whether conclusions would change substantially with alternative similarity metrics (e.g., DINO embeddings, human perceptual studies, or metrics from other vision-language models).

The paper's treatment of what constitutes "true" OOD performance is somewhat unsatisfying. The authors acknowledge in the Discussion section that measuring "true OOD performance" would require removing all images of a particular domain from LAION, which would necessitate a domain classifier with near-perfect accuracy. However, this caveat somewhat undermines the central claim. If we cannot definitively establish what is truly OOD with respect to LAION, then the conclusion that "high train-test similarity is insufficient to explain CLIP's performance" is somewhat weaker than presented. The authors are essentially showing that removing images more similar than ImageNet's threshold still leaves CLIP with good performance, but this doesn't necessarily prove that the removed images weren't driving performance—it only shows they weren't the sole driver.

The paper's scope is limited to zero-shot classification tasks. While this is a natural starting point, it's unclear whether the findings generalize to few-shot learning, fine-tuning, or other downstream tasks that have been central to CLIP's success. The paper doesn't explore whether the same similarity gap framework would apply in these settings, or whether other factors might dominate in different task regimes.

The analysis of what actually drives CLIP's generalization remains somewhat open-ended. The paper effectively demonstrates that high train-test similarity is not the primary factor, but the Discussion section (particularly the "What drives generalization?" paragraph) acknowledges that "a large chunk of performance remains to be explained." While the authors mention data diversity and density as likely factors, they don't investigate these systematically. The paper is somewhat stronger as a negative result (showing what doesn't explain performance) than as a positive contribution (showing what does).

The interpretation of the 30 percentage point performance gap (44.78% on ImageNet-Sketch vs. expected 14% for ImageNet-trained models at similar ImageNet-Val performance) is compelling but relies on the effective robustness line from Fang et al. (2022). The paper doesn't explore how sensitive this conclusion is to the specific reference model or whether different architectures or training procedures might yield different effective robustness curves.

## Detailed Comments and Questions

**On the similarity metric:** While Appendix E provides an ablation comparing ViT-B/16+ and ViT-L-14 embeddings, have the authors considered other vision-language models or vision-only models? For instance, how would results change using DINO, supervised ViT models, or embeddings from other vision-language models like ALIGN or LiT? This would strengthen claims about the robustness of the similarity metric choice.

**On experimental scope:** The authors justify focusing on ImageNet-based benchmarks in Appendix C, but the justification relies on arguments about human alignment and CLIP

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.
reviews.questions: What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: "Does CLIP's Generalization Performance Mainly Stem from High Train-Test Similarity?"

## Summary

This paper investigates the generalization behavior of CLIP from a novel and important perspective: whether the model's exceptional out-of-distribution (OOD) performance stems primarily from the presence of highly similar training samples in the web-scale LAION dataset. The authors address this fundamental question by systematically filtering LAION based on perceptual similarity metrics derived from CLIP embeddings, creating pruned subsets that match ImageNet-1K's similarity gap to various OOD benchmarks. Through extensive experiments, the paper demonstrates that CLIP maintains surprisingly strong performance even when trained on datasets with ImageNet-like similarity distributions to test sets, suggesting that factors beyond high train-test similarity are responsible for CLIP's remarkable generalization capabilities. A particularly noteworthy contribution is the identification of a 100M-sample core set of LAION-400M that maintains near-baseline performance, effectively reducing the training data to one-quarter of its original size.

## Strengths

1. **Novel and well-motivated research perspective**: This paper addresses an important and underexplored question about foundation models that has significant implications for our understanding of deep learning generalization. The focus on train-test similarity as a potential explanation for CLIP's performance is intuitive and well-justified, particularly given that LAION is a web-scale dataset that could plausibly contain samples similar to common OOD benchmarks. The authors make a compelling initial case in Figure 1, showing visual evidence of highly similar neighbors in LAION compared to ImageNet-Train, which effectively motivates the subsequent analysis.

2. **Rigorous experimental design and methodology**: The paper presents a well-structured experimental pipeline that progresses logically from establishing the correlation between nearest-neighbor similarity and performance (Section 4.2), to comparing similarity distributions across datasets (Section 4.3), to formalizing the notion of "highly similar images" through the similarity gap framework (Section 4.4). The formalization of the similarity gap concept is particularly elegant, providing a principled way to control for dataset size differences when comparing similarity distributions. The pruning methodology is sound and allows for controlled interventions that isolate the effect of highly similar samples.

3. **Compelling empirical findings with important implications**: The core result that CLIP maintains high performance even after removing highly similar samples is striking and counterintuitive. The observation in Section 6 that a model trained on sketch-pruned data achieves 44.78% accuracy on ImageNet-Sketch (compared to the expected ~14% based on ImageNet-trained models) represents a 30 percentage point improvement that cannot be explained by train-test similarity alone. This finding challenges conventional wisdom about the importance of dataset similarity and opens new research directions into understanding what properties of large-scale datasets actually drive generalization.

4. **Comprehensive experimental validation and supplementary analyses**: Beyond the main results, the paper includes extensive ablations and supplementary experiments that strengthen the conclusions. The analysis in Appendix B.2 examining MNIST and SVHN (non-ImageNet-like datasets) is particularly valuable, as it demonstrates that near-pruning effects are not universal but rather specific to the similarity metric used. The comparison between CLIP and ResNet18 performance under pruning (Appendix B.3) further validates that the observed effects are specific to vision-language models rather than a general property of neural networks. The visualization of nearest neighbors (Appendix G) provides qualitative support for the quantitative findings.

5. **Practical contribution through dataset curation**: The identification and release of a 100M core set (¼ of LAION's original size) that maintains performance is a valuable practical contribution that could facilitate future research on foundation model training. This core set, created by far-pruning with respect to six OOD benchmarks simultaneously, could serve as a more efficient alternative for researchers with computational constraints. The comparison in Table 3 showing that this core set outperforms similarly-sized datasets created through other deduplication methods (like SemDeDup) adds credibility to the curation approach.

6. **Thorough documentation and reproducibility**: The paper provides extensive implementation details in Section 3 and throughout the appendices, including training hyperparameters, similarity computation methods, and handling of duplicates. The authors have released code and datasets to enable reproduction and future work, which is commendable for a paper making strong empirical claims. The Reproducibility Statement explicitly directs readers to relevant sections, demonstrating a commitment to transparency.

## Weaknesses

1. **Limited scope of evaluation domains**: While the paper provides thorough analysis on ImageNet-based classification benchmarks, this represents a relatively narrow slice of CLIP's application domains and capabilities. The paper focuses almost exclusively on zero-shot classification tasks, but CLIP is equally important for image-text retrieval, which is arguably one of the most fundamental applications of vision-language models. The authors briefly acknowledge this limitation in Appendix C but do not adequately address it. A more comprehensive evaluation could include: (a) image-text retrieval benchmarks using MS-COCO as an in-distribution dataset and Flickr-30K as OOD, (b) diverse benchmarks from VTAB (Visual Task Adaptation Benchmark) as mentioned in the DataComp paper, (c) domain-specific evaluation on medical imaging or remote sensing tasks, and (d) fine-grained classification tasks where similarity might play a different role. The justification provided in Appendix C—that ImageNet-based benchmarks are "more human aligned"—is somewhat subjective and does not fully justify the narrow evaluation scope. Different application domains may exhibit very different relationships between train-test similarity and generalization performance.

2. **Incomplete analysis of the full similarity distribution**: While the paper compares the nearest neighbors between LAION and ImageNet-Train, a critical gap remains in the analysis. The authors show that the closest sample in LAION to test sets is roughly as far away as the closest sample in ImageNet-Train (after pruning), but they do not thoroughly analyze the density of samples in the similarity range just below this threshold. It is conceivable that even though both datasets have similar nearest-neighbor distances, LAION could contain substantially more samples with intermediate similarity values (e.g., similarity scores between 0.6 and 0.8) compared to ImageNet-Train. This would represent an "unfair advantage" where LAION has a richer continuum of similar samples even if the absolute nearest neighbor is at the same distance. While Appendix F addresses this concern to some extent through the analysis of normalized similarity distributions and Table 6, the main paper would benefit from a more prominent discussion of this point. The finding that LAION-pruned splits contain 5-20 times more samples near the similarity gap boundary is interesting but somewhat buried in the appendices and deserves more emphasis in the main narrative.

3. **Missing critical experimental details in the main text**: Several important methodological choices are relegated to the appendices or mentioned only briefly, making it difficult for readers to fully evaluate the methodology without extensive appendix consultation. Specifically: (a) The construction of nearest neighbor sets in Section 4.1 lacks detail—how exactly are the visualizations in Figures 1 and 2 generated, and what is the computational process? (b) The deduplication process for generating LAION-200M (Section 3) references Abbas et al. (2023) but does not provide sufficient detail about the specific thresholds and criteria used. This is important because the choice of deduplication threshold could affect downstream results. (c) The caption quality and format of ImageNet-Train samples when added to LAION (Section 5) is mentioned only briefly ("a photo of a {object class}"), but this design choice could significantly impact results since caption quality is known to affect CLIP training. A sensitivity analysis on caption format would strengthen the claims. (d) The specific embedding model and its training details (CLIP ViT-B/16+) are mentioned but the paper does not discuss whether results would generalize to embeddings from other vision-language models or different CLIP variants.

4. **Limited exploration of alternative explanations for remaining performance**: While the paper effectively rules out high train-test similarity as the primary driver of CLIP's performance, the discussion of alternative factors remains somewhat superficial. The authors mention "data diversity and density" as likely candidates (Section 6) but do not empirically investigate these factors. For instance: (a) How does the diversity of samples near the similarity gap threshold compare between LAION and ImageNet? (b) Do LAION samples in this region exhibit different stylistic or semantic diversity compared to ImageNet? (c) Could CLIP's language supervision play a more significant role than acknowledged, since LAION provides rich text descriptions while ImageNet-Train uses simple captions? (d) The paper does not investigate whether compositionality (mentioned briefly in Section 6) could explain the gap—can CLIP generalize from "sketches of cats and natural images of dogs to understanding sketches of dogs"? Empirical investigation of these factors would provide a more complete understanding of what drives CLIP's generalization.

5. **Potential circularity in using CLIP embeddings to evaluate CLIP similarity**: The paper uses

However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:

2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD.

Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior out-of-distribution (OOD) performance of foundation models like CLIP stems primarily from the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. To investigate this question, the authors systematically create several pruned splits of the LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. In their initial analysis, they demonstrate that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in OOD performance, which at first suggests that similarity is important. However, through a more careful analysis where they match the train-test similarity distribution with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet-pretrained model. This key finding suggests that although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this similarity is not the primary reason for CLIP's better OOD generalization. Understanding the fundamental reasons for better generalization of CLIP remains an important open question that this work attempts to address.

## Strengths

- **Valuable empirical contribution**: The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets through the similarity gap methodology, there is still a substantial improvement in test set performance compared to ImageNet-trained models is genuinely helpful for the community. Specifically, the observation that a CLIP model trained on 'sketch-pruned' data achieves 44.78% accuracy on ImageNet-Sketch while an ImageNet-trained model at the same validation accuracy would achieve only ~14% (according to effective robustness curves) is striking and provides strong evidence that similarity alone cannot explain CLIP's performance.

- **Comprehensive experimental investigation**: The paper presents several insightful experimental results that systematically build understanding of the relationship between training data and OOD performance. The progression from simple correlation analysis (Fig. 3 left) to similarity-based pruning experiments (Fig. 3 center/right) to the more sophisticated similarity gap framework (Section 4.4) demonstrates careful experimental design. These results are particularly valuable given the significant computational cost of retraining CLIP multiple times on different dataset splits.

- **Methodological rigor in similarity analysis**: The formalization of the similarity gap concept in Section 4.4 and Figure 5 provides a principled way to compare two training distributions while controlling for their different scales and densities. This is a non-trivial contribution that goes beyond simple nearest-neighbor analysis and allows for more meaningful comparisons between LAION and ImageNet.

- **Practical resource for the community**: The release of pruned datasets and especially the 100M core set of LAION (described in Section 4.2 and Appendix B.4) that maintains CLIP's OOD performance while being 1/4 the original size is a valuable contribution that can facilitate future research. This core set could serve as a useful benchmark for investigating data properties that drive generalization.

- **Thorough ablations and supplementary analysis**: The paper includes extensive ablation studies and supplementary experiments. The comparison between different embedding metrics (Appendix E, Table 5) showing strong correlation (ρS > 0.80) between ViT-B/16+ and ViT-L-14 embeddings validates the choice of similarity metric. The analysis on non-ImageNet datasets (Appendix D) with CelebA and Waterbirds demonstrates that the method is dataset-agnostic and the findings generalize beyond ImageNet-based benchmarks.

## Major Weaknesses

### Primary Concern: Insufficient for True OOD Testing

Although the results are interesting and the experimental work is substantial, my primary concern is that the analysis, as currently framed, is **not sufficient to enable fair OOD testing**. This is a fundamental issue that affects the interpretation of the main claims.

The core problem lies in the distinction between **in-distribution (ID)** and **out-of-distribution (OOD)** evaluation. If the paper were focused solely on ID performance, the approach of removing individual training samples that are similar to each test sample would be sufficient and appropriate. However, OOD evaluation fundamentally requires that the **distribution** of images in the test domain is unknown or unseen during training. To truly achieve OOD evaluation, all images from the test **domain** (not just those similar to specific test samples) should ideally be removed from the training set.

**The critical flaw in the current approach**: The similarity gap methodology removes only those training samples that are more similar to the test set than any ImageNet-Train sample. This means:

1. **Incomplete domain removal**: Consider the ImageNet-Sketch benchmark. If there are 50 sketches of "airplane" in the test set, the pruning removes all training samples more similar to these specific sketches than ImageNet-Train samples are. However, there may be other airplane sketches in LAION that are farther away than the closest ImageNet-Train image to the test sketches. These other sketches would not be removed, even though they belong to the same "sketch" domain. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of a given class and test domain are removed from the training set.

2. **Unexamined domain examples**: More problematically, there may be sketches of objects (such as "space shuttle") that are not included in ImageNet at all. These sketches could be present in LAION alongside natural images of space shuttles. Under the current pruning scheme, such sketches might not be removed if they happen to be dissimilar enough to the test sketches. This means the "sketch" domain is not truly unseen by CLIP, and the evaluation cannot be claimed to be genuinely OOD.

3. **Partial domain coverage**: The paper acknowledges this limitation in Section 6 ("Measuring the true OOD performance"), where the authors state: "Another interesting analysis would be to prune LAION images to measure its true OOD performance. To remove all images of a certain domain, we need to be able to label each image as 'ID' or 'OOD'." However, the fact that this analysis is deferred to future work is problematic because it means the current conclusions about OOD generalization are not as strong as claimed.

**Implications for interpretation**: While the paper's findings about the relationship between similarity and performance are still valuable, they should be interpreted more carefully. The work demonstrates that **per-sample similarity** is not the sole driver of CLIP's performance, but it does not definitively answer whether **domain-level similarity** (i.e., the presence of the entire sketch domain, rendered images domain, etc. in the training data) explains CLIP's OOD performance.

### Secondary Concern: Incomplete Analysis of Domain Structure

Related to the above, the paper does not fully investigate whether the pruned datasets actually achieve domain separation. For example:

- **Cross-domain contamination**: The paper does not systematically verify that domain-specific features (e.g., sketch-like patterns, rendering artifacts) have been fully removed from the pruned training sets. While Figure 21-26 in Appendix G.2 show nearest neighbors after pruning, these are only visualizations of a small sample and do not provide quantitative assurance that domain-specific features are absent.

- **Style transfer effects**: CLIP is trained with language supervision, which means it learns compositional features. It's possible that even with per-sample similarity pruning, CLIP can still learn to generalize to sketches by composing features from sketches of other classes (as the authors themselves acknowledge in the "Does compositionality drive performance?" discussion in Section 6). The pruning approach does not directly address whether compositional generalization from sketch examples of other classes enables performance on sketch examples of the test classes.

## Minor Weaknesses

- **Clarity of presentation**: The abstract and contributions list could be more concise and clearer about what is actually being claimed. The current framing uses "out-of-distribution generalization" throughout, but as discussed above, the paper does not fully achieve true OOD testing. It would be more accurate to describe the work as investigating whether **per-sample similarity** to test sets drives CLIP's performance, rather than making broader claims about OOD generalization.

- **Terminology**: The term "similarity gap" in Section 4.4 and throughout could be confusing, as "generalization gap" is a well-established term in machine learning with a different meaning (the difference between training and test error). While the authors do define their usage clearly, a different term (e.g., "similarity hull" or "similarity boundary") might reduce confusion.

- **Limited theoretical insight**: While the empirical results are valuable, the paper provides limited theoretical insight into what properties of LAION (beyond scale and diversity) actually drive generalization. The discussion in Section 6 acknowledges this ("Many distribution properties could contribute to generalization performance..."), but more concrete hypotheses or analyses of candidate properties would strengthen the work.

- **Incomplete comparison with related work**: The paper could better position itself relative to concurrent work on data properties and CLIP's generalization. For instance, Gadre et al. (2023) on Data

reviews.summary: This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.
reviews.strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?
reviews.questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: "Does CLIP's Generalization Performance Mainly Stem from High Train-Test Similarity?"

## Summary

This paper investigates the hypothesis that CLIP's exceptional performance on out-of-distribution (OOD) benchmarks stems primarily from the presence of highly similar images in its large-scale training dataset (LAION-400M). The authors employ a systematic approach by first quantifying perceptual similarity using CLIP's image embedding space, then demonstrating that OOD benchmarks like ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R contain more semantically and stylistically similar neighbors in LAION than in ImageNet-Train. Their central contribution is a pruning methodology based on the concept of "similarity gaps" that removes training samples more similar to test sets than any ImageNet-Train sample. Surprisingly, models trained on these pruned datasets maintain relatively high performance, leading the authors to conclude that high train-test similarity alone cannot fully explain CLIP's generalization capabilities. Additionally, they identify and release a 100M-sample core set of LAION that maintains full OOD performance.

## Strengths

**Clear and Compelling Visual Evidence**

Figure 1 provides compelling visual evidence that LAION-400M contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks, including near-duplicates, while ImageNet-Train contains relatively dissimilar samples. This visual demonstration is intuitive and effectively motivates the research question. The presentation of query images alongside their nearest neighbors in both datasets makes the phenomenon immediately apparent and convincing to readers.

**Quantitative Validation of Perceptual Similarity**

Figure 3 (left) rigorously quantifies the relationship between nearest-neighbor similarity and CLIP's zero-shot performance, showing a strong positive correlation across multiple datasets. The fact that test samples with similarity scores near 1.0 achieve substantially higher accuracy (69% for ImageNet-Sketch) compared to those with lower similarity (35%) provides robust quantitative support for the importance of this metric. This correlation analysis is well-executed and provides a principled foundation for subsequent experiments.

**Systematic Pruning Methodology**

Figure 3 (right) demonstrates through controlled experiments that near-pruning (removing similar samples first) causes much steeper performance degradation than random pruning, while far-pruning (removing dissimilar samples) maintains or even slightly improves performance. This experimental design effectively isolates the causal role of similarity by comparing three distinct pruning strategies. The observation that far-pruning performance remains stable until 100M samples are removed is particularly noteworthy and suggests that many training samples may not contribute meaningfully to OOD generalization.

**Rigorous Formalization of the Similarity Gap**

The formalization of "highly similar images" through the similarity gap concept (Section 4.4) is mathematically sound and provides a principled way to compare training distributions. The authors clearly define how to align the similarity distributions of two datasets and provide a visual illustration (Figure 5) that makes the abstract concept concrete. This formalization allows for precise, reproducible comparisons across different datasets and test sets.

**Comprehensive Experimental Coverage**

The paper evaluates the hypothesis across six different ImageNet-based OOD benchmarks (ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R, ImageNet-A, ImageNet-V2, ObjectNet, and ImageNet-Val), plus additional datasets like CelebA and Waterbirds in the appendix. This breadth of evaluation strengthens the generalizability of the findings. The authors also provide extensive ablations, including sensitivity to embedding choice (Appendix E) and analysis on non-ImageNet datasets (Appendix B.2), which demonstrate thoroughness in their experimental approach.

**Practical Contribution of the Core Set**

The identification and release of a 100M-sample core set (roughly 1/4 of LAION's original size) that maintains CLIP's full OOD performance is a valuable practical contribution. This could facilitate future research by reducing computational requirements while maintaining performance, making it easier for researchers to study the relationship between training data properties and generalization.

**Honest Discussion of Limitations**

The authors acknowledge important limitations of their work, including the vagueness in defining what constitutes a "domain" for true OOD evaluation (Section 6), the limitations of their similarity metric, and the open question of whether compositionality might explain remaining performance gaps. This intellectual honesty strengthens the paper's credibility.

## Weaknesses

**Lack of Critical Comparative Analysis with ImageNet-Trained Models**

While Figure 3 shows pruning effects on CLIP models, the paper does not adequately address a fundamental question: Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more rapidly than ImageNet-trained models when similar samples are removed? This is a critical omission because it would directly test whether CLIP's performance is uniquely dependent on similar samples compared to traditional supervised learning approaches. The authors briefly mention in Appendix B.3 that "near/far pruning affects CLIP performance more than ResNet18," but this finding deserves much more prominent discussion in the main paper with detailed analysis. If ImageNet-trained ResNet models show comparable or even greater sensitivity to pruning similar samples, it would substantially weaken the paper's central claim that CLIP's generalization is uniquely explained by high train-test similarity.

**Insufficient Analysis of Cross-Dataset Pruning Effects**

Figure 7 reveals a critical issue that receives inadequate discussion: pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch also substantially decreases performance on ImageNet-R and vice versa. While the authors briefly note that "there is some style overlap in ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R," this explanation is superficial. A more thorough analysis would investigate whether these pruned samples are important for understanding the general ImageNet distribution rather than being specific to individual benchmarks. This raises the possibility that the pruned samples contribute to a broader understanding of visual concepts rather than simply memorizing specific test sets. The cross-dataset effects suggest that the "highly similar images" may actually be capturing important general features of the visual world that support robust generalization.

**Unclear Motivation for Similarity Gap Correction in Section 5**

The paper's central motivation becomes muddled in Sections 5 and 6. The authors establish that pruning to match ImageNet's similarity gap results in only modest performance drops (Table 1), which they interpret as evidence that high train-test similarity is not the primary driver of CLIP's performance. However, the practical motivation for this exercise is unclear. Why should we want to prune training datasets to deliberately perform worse on test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? The authors do not adequately justify why this particular correction is meaningful or what insights it provides beyond the near/far-pruning experiments already presented in Section 4.2. The distinction between the unconstrained pruning in Section 4.2 and the constrained pruning in Section 4.4 needs clearer motivation.

**Potential Transductive Learning Concerns**

Section 5's methodology raises important questions about whether the models trained on pruned datasets should be considered as engaging in transductive learning rather than true zero-shot transfer. By explicitly pruning based on specific test sets, the training process becomes indirectly influenced by information about those test sets. This is particularly concerning given that some datasets like ObjectNet have explicit licenses prohibiting use of their samples to tune model parameters. While the authors do not literally include test samples in the training set, the pruning procedure creates a form of indirect test set knowledge in the training process. The paper would benefit from a more thorough discussion of whether this violates the spirit of zero-shot evaluation and how it relates to common evaluation practices in the community.

**Incomplete Analysis of What Replaces High Similarity in Driving Performance**

While the paper successfully argues that high train-test similarity is insufficient to explain CLIP's performance, it provides limited insight into what actually does drive the performance. The authors mention "data diversity and density" as potential factors (Section 6) but provide almost no empirical analysis of these properties. The observation that CLIP trained on 'sketch-pruned' data achieves 44.78% on ImageNet-Sketch compared to the 14% expected from ImageNet-Train at the same validation accuracy is striking, but the paper leaves this 30 percentage point gap largely unexplained. A more thorough investigation of potential explanatory factors—such as the diversity of visual concepts in LAION, the density of training samples in the feature space, or the compositional structure of the data—would strengthen the paper's contribution beyond simply ruling out one hypothesis.

**Limited Discussion of Compositionality and Feature Recombination**

The paper briefly mentions compositionality in Section 6 as an "open question" but does not develop this idea sufficiently. The hypothesis that CLIP can generalize from "sketches of cats and natural images of dogs to understanding sketches of dogs" is intriguing and potentially crucial to understanding the remaining performance gap. However, the paper provides no empirical evidence or detailed analysis of this mechanism. Given that this could explain a substantial portion of the performance gap, it deserves more rigorous investigation.

**Methodological Concern: Circular Use of CLIP Embeddings**

The paper uses CLIP's own image embeddings
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Simple Hierarchical Planning with Diffusion

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.
reviews.strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.
reviews.weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.
reviews.questions: See weakness above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
